name: Herron Mine
short_description: Historic gold mining location in central Georgia with accessible quartz and placer gold deposits. Explore where miners once worked the rich Piedmont gold belt.
description:
Herron Mine is a historic gold mining site in Upson County, representing Georgia's important role in the American Gold Rush of the 1830s. The Piedmont region of Georgia, where this mine is located, produced significant quantities of gold before larger discoveries out West shifted the prospecting focus. The site sits on relatively flat terrain at 761 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounders of varying experience levels.

The geology here reflects the metamorphic rocks of the Piedmont province. Ancient folded and faulted metamorphic rocks, particularly mica schist and quartzite formations, host small quartz veins and placer deposits that accumulated gold during weathering and erosion. The Herron Mine worked these lode deposits directly, extracting gold-bearing quartz from the host rock.

Today, collectors can search for small gold flakes, gold dust, and quartz fragments in the surrounding area. The flat terrain and accessible location make this a good site for learning about Georgia's mining heritage while practicing basic rockhounding skills. Success depends on searching carefully in mineralized zones and understanding the relationship between quartz outcrops and gold occurrence.

This location offers a direct connection to Georgia's early industrial history - the state produced over 1 million ounces of gold during the Piedmont Gold Rush era. While the major deposits are depleted, small-scale collection remains possible for patient rockhounders willing to investigate old workings and nearby weathered outcrops.
history: The Herron Mine operated during Georgia's Piedmont Gold Rush in the 1830s-1840s, when the discovery of gold in northern Georgia sparked the region's first major mining boom. The mine exploited lode gold deposits hosted in metamorphic rocks typical of the Piedmont province, where hydrothermal activity during the Alleghanian orogeny concentrated gold in quartz veins within mica schist and altered metamorphic host rocks.
tips: Search along exposed quartz veins and in the weathered decomposition products (saprolite) at the edges of old mine workings - this is where erosion and oxidation concentrate fine gold particles. Use a small gold pan to test material from around the site; even fine gold dust will show up quickly in a pan and confirm productive areas. Look for white quartz outcrops and follow them downslope where gravity has carried heavier gold particles; the transition zone between fresh bedrock and weathered material often produces the best results. Bring a hand lens and small chisel to examine quartz fragments for visible gold inclusions - the sight of actual gold encourages further searching.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain at 761 feet elevation. Typical Piedmont landscape with mixed deciduous forest, some open areas near historical workings. Walking distance from parking to collecting areas is minimal to moderate (under 1 mile). Footing is generally stable but may include uneven ground, exposed root systems, and weathered mine spoil piles. No significant elevation change. Well-drained sandy/clay soil typical of Georgia Piedmont.
best_season: March - May, September - November. These periods offer mild temperatures and low rainfall, making fieldwork comfortable. Summer heat and humidity can be intense in central Georgia; winter is cool and damp. Fall and spring provide optimal conditions for extended searching and detailed work around mineralized zones.
written_directions: From Yatesville, head south on GA-19 for approximately 3 miles. Turn onto local county roads toward the Herron Mine area near the Upson County mining district. The site is accessible via passenger vehicle on maintained roads, though final approach may be unpaved. GPS coordinates (32.918057, -84.139999) will guide you to the historic mining location. Contact local county authorities or historical societies for current access conditions and any seasonal closures.
road_conditions: Maintained county and state roads to the area. Final approach may be unpaved but passable by standard passenger vehicles. Check local conditions seasonally.
parking_notes: Historic mine site with informal parking near old workings. Limited designated spaces; park responsibly and do not block access roads.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Collect small amounts of quartz and gold for hobby rockhounding. Respect the historic nature of the site - do not disturb major mine structures or protected historical features. No commercial collection or sale of materials. Stay within designated collecting areas and do not expand excavations into unstable zones.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Respect historical structures
Stay in designated areas
Small-scale collection only
nearest_services: Yatesville (approximately 3 miles): Gas, food, and water available. Thomaston (larger town, 10 miles) offers more comprehensive services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families with young children. Unpaved roads in final approach but passable by standard vehicles. Some areas around old mine workings have loose rock and uneven ground - sturdy footwear recommended. Minimal elevation change makes this accessible to those with moderate mobility. Avoid steep mine pit areas and unstable excavations.

faq.question: Can I find gold at Herron Mine in Georgia?
faq.answer: Yes, Herron Mine is a historic lode gold site in Georgia's Piedmont gold belt. Small amounts of gold can still be found in quartz fragments, as fine gold dust, and in panned material from weathered zones around the old workings. The site operated during Georgia's 1830s-1840s Gold Rush, and patient rockhounders continue to recover small gold flakes today.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Yatesville, Georgia?
faq.answer: March through May and September through November offer ideal conditions at Herron Mine. These seasons provide mild temperatures and lower humidity compared to Georgia's hot, humid summers. Spring and fall weather makes extended field searching comfortable and productive for both beginners and experienced collectors.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect at Herron Mine?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal, non-commercial collecting at Herron Mine. Simply follow local collection guidelines: limit yourself to small hobby quantities, respect the historic structures at the site, and stay in designated collecting areas. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: How do I find gold in quartz at this Georgia mining location?
faq.answer: Focus on exposed quartz veins and weathered material (saprolite) at the edges of old mine workings where erosion concentrates gold. Use a small gold pan to test material - even fine dust will show in the pan. Search the transition zone between fresh bedrock and weathered surface material, where gravity has carried heavier gold particles downslope. A hand lens helps identify gold flecks in white quartz fragments.

faq.question: Is Herron Mine a good rockhounding site for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Herron Mine is beginner-friendly. The flat terrain requires no technical climbing, parking is convenient, and no permit is needed. The site offers an excellent introduction to Georgia's gold mining history while teaching practical skills like panning and identifying mineralized zones. Families and children ages 6 and up can participate safely with basic supervision.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should I bring to Herron Mine for gold panning?
faq.answer: Bring a small gold pan (12-14 inch is standard), a classifier or mesh screen, tweezers for picking out fine gold, and a small vial for storage. A hand lens or loupe helps identify gold in quartz. Wear sturdy shoes for uneven mine terrain, and bring water and sun protection. A small chisel or pick can help extract quartz samples from weathered outcrop areas.

meta_description: Find gold and quartz at historic Herron Mine near Yatesville, Georgia. No permit required, beginner-friendly collecting on public land in the Piedmont gold belt.
